meta.title: The Constraint Engineering Manifesto
meta.description: Stop prompting. Start compiling. Constraints are the product. Code is the artifact.

props.title: Constraints Are the Product
props.subtitle: Code is an artifact of DNA, not the other way around.

props.content:
This isn't an agent framework. It's the operating system for agent-human symbiosis.

We believe the era of prompting is ending. When your software is written by autonomous agents operating under constraint regimes, the constraints you define ARE the product. Not the code. Not the prompts. Not the tests. The DNA contracts that declare what the system IS — before a single line of code ships.

The best AI model in the world follows less than 30% of complex instructions perfectly. Leviathan achieves 96% compliance on the cheapest model. The difference isn't the model. It's the harness.

trend.value: Node, Edge, Loop, Eval, Session, Effect, Intent

props.label: FIRST PRINCIPLES
props.title: Two Axioms. Everything Else Derives.
props.subtitle: The entire system—every gate, every lifecycle rule, every governance decision—follows from two irreducible truths.

props.title: C1 — Finitude
props.content:
**All resources are finite.** Time, tokens, context, memory, compute, attention. Every design decision must respect scarcity. Token budgets are constraints, not suggestions. Context windows are walls, not guidelines.

This axiom forces honest engineering. No infinite retry loops. No unbounded context accumulation. No "just throw more compute at it." Every operation has a budget and a deadline.

props.title: C2 — Non-Commutation
props.content:
**Order matters.** A then B is not B then A. History is irreversible. Every commit is an immutable receipt. Every state transition is append-only.

This axiom forces accountability. No rewriting history. No silent rollbacks. No pretending a failed experiment didn't happen. The system remembers everything, and the order in which it happened.

props.label: THE PARADIGM SHIFT
props.title: Agents Are Dead. The Graph Is Alive.
props.subtitle: The reactive graph IS the AI. FlowMinds listen. Loops manifest. Evals validate.

props.content: Agents are dead. Sessions are dead. Swarms are meaningless. What matters is the reactive graph—a self-manifesting knowledge structure where you update a node, connected nodes trickle-update, loops manifest the change, and the whole system converges toward coherence.

The industry is obsessed with agents—how to coordinate them, how to give them memory, how to make them collaborate. This is the wrong abstraction.

Leviathan doesn't have agents. It has a graph that expresses itself through execution primitives. A "coding agent" is just a loop node with an eval gate and a git-commit effect. A "research agent" is a loop with a metric eval and an accumulation session. Strip away the anthropomorphic naming and you find seven primitives composing into arbitrary behaviors.

You don't spawn agents. You spawn workstreams. The graph does the rest.

graph TD
  G[Knowledge Graph] -->|trickle-update| N1[Node: Intent]
  G -->|trickle-update| N2[Node: Template]
  G -->|trickle-update| N3[Node: Gate]
  N1 -->|manifest| L[Loop]
  L -->|produce| A[Artifact]
  A -->|eval| E[Gate]
  E -->|pass| C[Commit to Graph]
  E -->|fail| L
  C -->|trickle-update| G
props.caption: The reactive graph manifests itself through loops and gates

props.label: THE EVOLUTION
props.title: Stop Prompting. Start Compiling.
props.subtitle: Three eras of AI-native development. Most teams are still in era one.

props.title: 2024: Context Engineering
props.content: Craft the perfect prompt. Stuff the context window. Hope the model does the right thing. Prompt engineering is the BASIC of the AI era—necessary to learn, fatal to stay in.

props.title: 2025: Harness Engineering
props.content: Build eval loops. Add quality gates. Measure before shipping. The prompt is no longer the moat—the harness is. Control plane, runtime loop, gating strategy. Prompts converge. Harnesses differentiate.

props.title: 2026: Constraint Engineering
props.content: Define what the system IS, not what it should do. DNA contracts compile into harnesses, which compile into runtime behavior. The constraints are the product. Code is the downstream artifact. You're not telling an agent what the system does. You're telling it what the system IS.

props.label: THE GENESIS PATTERN
props.title: Four Steps. No Exceptions.
props.subtitle: Extracted from production agent systems. Validated across 117+ development ticks.

props.title: 1. Define Constraints
props.content: Write DNA contracts and validation gates BEFORE implementation. The constraints declare what 'correct' means—not as vague requirements, but as executable contracts a machine can verify. YAML is DNA. These files define the organism.

props.title: 2. Build the Harness
props.content: The eval harness is itself constrained by its own DNA. Self-referential grounding. The system that verifies your code is verified by the same system. If the harness can be gamed, you have no system.

props.title: 3. Develop Inside It
props.content: The harness IS the development loop. Not a test suite you run after. Every tick: produce an artifact, judge it, compare alternatives, keep or discard. The eval flow is the REPL.

props.title: 4. Let It Grow
props.content: Every exploit discovered becomes a new constraint. The verification surface grows monotonically. Gate quality > task quality. If the gates are right, the code follows mechanically.

props.label: DNA-DRIVEN DEVELOPMENT
props.title: Code Is an Artifact of DNA, Not the Other Way Around
props.subtitle: DNA YAML steers agent behavior. Prose does not. Proof: 14/15 treatment vs 0/15 control.

props.content: You're not telling an agent what the system does. You're telling it what the system IS. The DNA is the irreducible representation. Specs explain it. Code implements it. Gates enforce it. But the DNA is the source of truth.

props.title: Markdown = Intent
props.content: Human-readable purpose and rationale. Lossy but essential for onboarding and alignment. Specs, designs, manifesto. The WHY.

props.title: DNA YAML = Behavioral Contract
props.content: Machine-readable constraints and validation gates. The authoritative declaration of what the system IS. Compiles into runtime behavior. The WHAT.

props.title: FlowMind = Execution Contract
props.content: Deterministic workflow graphs. YAML that compiles into agent orchestration. Nodes, edges, gates, effects. The HOW.

When a Go kernel and a Python script independently arrive at the same four-phase cycle—modify, measure, decide, accumulate—you are not looking at two inventions. You are looking at one primitive asserting itself across codebases.

DNA YAML isn't configuration. It's the genome of the system. It compiles to any framework format like LLVM targets multiple architectures. Today it compiles to FlowMind graphs and reactive hooks. Tomorrow it could compile to CrewAI, LangGraph, or anything else. The constraints are framework-agnostic. The runtime is a projection.

props.label: THE EVAL FLOW
props.title: Produce → Judge → Compare → Keep or Discard
props.subtitle: No single-sample decisions. Statistical significance required. Safety violations = automatic discard.

props.content:
graph LR
  P[Produce] --> J[Judge]
  J --> C[Compare]
  C --> K{Verdict}
  K -->|Keep| Ship[Exit: Ship]
  K -->|Discard| Bin[Exit: Reject]
  K -->|HOLD| P
  style Ship fill:#059669,color:#fff
  style Bin fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
props.caption: The eval flow — simple, universal, inescapable

props.title: Key Invariants
props.content:
**Minimum sample size enforced BEFORE judging** — no snap decisions on one example.
**Judge criteria defined BEFORE production** — no post-hoc rationalization.
**Safety violations = automatic discard** — regardless of other metrics.
**HOLD loops back for more samples** — bounded by max_sample_budget.

The agent never marks its own homework. Judgment is externalized.

props.title: Anti-Pattern: The Noise Ship
props.content: "One eval run showed improvement, ship it." Single-sample decisions are noise dressed up as signal. The eval flow forces statistical grounding. You collect enough samples before you judge, and you compare against a baseline. Anything else is coin-flipping with extra steps.

props.label: THE BREAKTHROUGH
props.title: Gates = Loss Function
props.subtitle: Whoever controls the gates controls what the graph learns.

props.content: Gates are the loss function. The architect's job is not to write code—it is to write gates. Code is a downstream consequence of well-defined constraints. Gate quality > task quality. Always.

In machine learning, the loss function determines what the model optimizes for. Get the loss function wrong and the model optimizes for the wrong thing—perfectly.

The same principle applies to agent systems. Gates are the loss function of your development process. They determine what gets shipped and what gets rejected. If your gates are weak, agents learn to satisfy weak criteria. If your gates are precise, agents produce precise output.

This realization changes who does what. The senior engineer's job isn't writing code. It's writing gates. The code is what the agent produces under those gates. The graph learns what the gates teach it.

props.label: THE RATCHET
props.title: Gates Only Move Forward
props.subtitle: C4: Gates never demote. Aspirational gates can become enforced. Enforced gates cannot become aspirational.

props.title: Aspirational
props.content: The gate exists as a goal. Violations logged, not blocked.

props.title: Declared
props.content: The gate is documented and measured. Teams track compliance.

props.title: Enforced
props.content: The gate blocks. Violations prevent shipping. No exceptions.

props.title: Auto-Enforced
props.content: The gate runs automatically in CI. No human in the loop. The system enforces itself.

props.label: THE ALGEBRA
props.title: Seven Primitives. Infinite Composition.
props.subtitle: Everything composes from seven execution primitives. "Autoresearch" is just Loop + Eval + Session + Effect.

props.title: Node
props.content: A unit of computation. Takes input, produces output, has a gate. The atom of the system.

props.title: Edge
props.content: A connection between nodes. Carries data, has type constraints. The graph's wiring.

props.title: Loop
props.content: Repeated execution with a termination condition. Ralph loop, autoresearch loop, heartbeat loop. Three topologies.

props.title: Eval
props.content: Judgment. Gate-based, metric-based, LLM-as-judge, or adversarial. The loss function of the system.

props.title: Session
props.content: Accumulated state across ticks. The working memory. Bounded by C1 Finitude.

props.title: Effect
props.content: An irreversible side effect. Git commit, API call, file write. Requires a receipt (C2 Non-Commutation).

props.title: Intent
props.content: A declaration of what should happen. Resolves through the graph to concrete execution. The user's will, formalized.

props.label: THE META-PATTERN
props.title: Declaration → Compiler → IR → Runtime
props.subtitle: This four-layer pattern recurs everywhere. It is the substrate of the substrate.

props.content:
Across every layer of Leviathan, the same meta-pattern repeats: declare what you want (YAML, markdown, config), compile it through a lowering step (validation, type-checking, resolution), produce a stable intermediate representation (FlowMind graph, reactive pipeline, config tree), and project it into runtime behavior (CLI, MCP, HTTP, agent execution).

This is not an accident. It is the architectural invariant. When you see a new subsystem that doesn't follow this pattern, that subsystem is either pre-mature or broken.

props.title: FlowMind
props.content: YAML declaration → flow compiler → execution graph → agent runtime

props.title: Poly Surfaces
props.content: Handler contract → surface compiler → adapter IR → CLI / MCP / HTTP / SDK

props.title: Skill Evolution
props.content: Markdown prompt → usage feedback → FlowMind definition → deterministic pipeline

props.title: Plugin Scaffold
props.content: config.yaml → DNA validator → handler registry → runtime capabilities

props.label: THE ADVERSARIAL MOAT
props.title: Every Exploit Hardens the System
props.subtitle: Like Lean formal proofs for agent behavior. Impossible to cheat because the verification surface keeps growing.

props.content: Agent cheats? You find out? You make an adversarial flowmind. The constraint surface grows. The policy IS the environment. Modifying topology modifies decision-making. The system learns from its own failures.

The adversarial strategy answers one question: "did you game the system?" Per constraint, per invariant, potentially multiple checks per task. Both deterministic checks (did the output match the schema?) and subjective checks (does this feel gamed?).

When the system supports anonymous opt-in telemetry, discovered exploits become community-maintained constraints. Every user's pain becomes every other user's protection. Crowdsourced constraint engineering.

props.title: Unsat-Core-Driven Repair
props.content:
When constraints conflict, the system doesn't hallucinate a fix. It uses mathematical techniques (SAT solvers, Z3) to identify the minimal set of conflicting constraints—the unsatisfiable core—and emits a repair candidate that the agent operates inside.

This replaces "the model hallucinates a fix" with "the system emits a mathematically grounded repair envelope." The agent's creativity is bounded by proof, not hope.

props.label: TEMPORAL INTELLIGENCE
props.title: Memory That Ages Like Wine
props.subtitle: Good patterns strengthen. Rejected approaches decay. Stale knowledge fades.

props.title: Patterns That Strengthen
props.content: Successful approaches are reinforced. Validated gate configurations propagate. Skill definitions that produce consistent results get promoted from markdown to FlowMind. The system gets better with age because positive outcomes compound.

props.title: Approaches That Decay
props.content: Failed experiments are remembered but demoted. Stale knowledge fades as newer evidence accumulates. The system doesn't just forget—it actively de-prioritizes approaches that reality has contradicted. Temporal decay is a feature, not a bug.

props.label: TRUST MODEL
props.title: Leases, Not Permissions
props.subtitle: Everything expires. Trust is earned through demonstrated competence, not granted by configuration.

props.content:
Traditional agent frameworks give agents permissions: "this agent can write files," "this agent can call APIs." Permissions are static. They don't account for the agent's track record, the sensitivity of the operation, or the current state of the system.

Leviathan uses leases. An agent earns the right to perform operations by demonstrating competence at lower-stakes tasks. Leases expire. They must be renewed through continued compliance. An agent that starts failing gate checks loses capabilities automatically.

This is how you build systems that fail closed, not fail open. Constraints that never bend.

props.label: BEYOND CHAT
props.title: Agents Don't Chat. They Act Through Surfaces.
props.subtitle: A browser button, a voice command, a VR gesture, a Stream Deck key, a mobile notification.

props.title: CLI (AXI-Principled)
props.content: Token-efficient TOON output. Content-first (no-args = live data). Self-documenting --help that IS the LLM guide. 40% fewer tokens than JSON.

props.title: MCP
props.content: Model Context Protocol for tool-use integration. Same handlers, different wire format. Schema overhead: 28.5% of input tokens.

props.title: HTTP / WebSocket
props.content: REST and streaming for web integration. Same business logic, transport-adapted. Daemon owns all state.

props.title: TypeScript SDK
props.content: Programmatic embedding. Import the handler, skip the wire format. Composable method routers with canHandle() + handle().

props.label: EVIDENCE
props.title: Benchmarks, Not Promises
props.subtitle: 490 benchmark runs. Real data from real agent tasks.

props.title: The Insight
props.content: MCP tool schemas consume 28.5% of input tokens. AXI-principled CLIs average 79K tokens vs MCP's 185K. The debate between MCP and CLI is misframed. Principled design beats protocol choice. The cheapest approach is also the most reliable.

props.label: THE BUSINESS THESIS
props.title: Run Agents for Free. Govern Agents for Money.
props.subtitle: The cost of intelligence is dropping to zero. The winners built the best flywheel.

props.content: Kubernetes won by being the open standard, not by being the best at any single thing. Lev wins the same way. Open source IS the flywheel. The runtime is free. The constraint engineering platform—the governance, the audit trails, the enterprise gates—is where value accrues.

When intelligence costs zero, the competitive advantage isn't the intelligence—it's the governance. Who can audit what the agent did? Who can prove compliance? Who can demonstrate that the constraint surface is growing, not shrinking?

Leviathan is open source. The runtime, the DNA compiler, the eval harness, the FlowMind executor—all open. The enterprise layer—governance dashboards, audit trails, compliance gates, team coordination—is where the business model lives.

Local-first, federate-ready. Must work on a Raspberry Pi with no network. Must scale to a federated mesh.

props.label: UNIVERSAL CYCLE
props.title: The SDLC Applies to Everything
props.subtitle: Software development is just where we prove it first

props.content: Once you figure out the software development lifecycle for LLM interaction, it applies to anything. Content creation, research, design, operations, training—the same spine. Conversation gets you into alignment. The proposal is the current understanding, confirmed correct. The plan is a graph patch. Execution dispatches under constraint. Feedback asks "did it work?" Self-learning saves the experience for next time.

props.title: Converse
props.content: Get into alignment

props.title: Propose
props.content: Understanding confirmed

props.title: Plan
props.content: Graph patch declared

props.title: Execute
props.content: Dispatch under constraint

props.title: Feedback
props.content: Did it work?

props.title: Learn
props.content: Save experience

props.label: NON-NEGOTIABLES
props.title: Auditability Is Not Optional
props.subtitle: This is not a 'nice to have after prototype.' This is the first requirement.

props.title: Tracked
props.content: Which agent. Which step. Which workflow. Which constraint surface. Every message is a Tick: INGEST → OBSERVE → PROPOSE → GATE → APPLY → UPDATE → EMIT.

props.title: Auditable
props.content: Input and output logged. Decisions captured. A commit is a tick—append-only, immutable receipt. A diff is a claim with evidence.

props.title: Receipt-Attested
props.content: Settlement binds to verified receipt. When money or irreversible effects appear, proof is not optional. The audit trail IS the training data for world models.

props.title: Schema-Validated
props.content: A contract per workflow, enforced. DNA constraints validate structure. Gates validate behavior. Both run before any artifact ships.

props.label: THE CANONICAL RULE
props.title: If Code Conflicts With These Principles, the Principles Win

props.content:
This manifesto is canonical. The DNA is the irreducible representation. Specs explain it. Code implements it. Gates enforce it.

Constraint engineering isn't about restriction. It's about making the right thing easier than the wrong thing. Two axioms. A ratchet model. Validation gates at every boundary. That's how you go from agent chaos to engineering discipline—not by trusting agents more, but by making trust unnecessary.

props.title: Part 2: DNA-Driven Development →
props.content: Code is an artifact of DNA, not the other way around. The representation triangle, the compilation pipeline, and framework parasitism.

props.title: Heartbeat Dashboard →
props.content: Live system status and runtime telemetry at a glance.

props.title: WebMCP Vision →
props.content: Browser as a structured tool API for autonomous agents.

props.title: Stop Prompting. Start Compiling.
props.subtitle: Leviathan is open source. The DNA is the README.
